3: Large amounts of animal slurry are produced in Europe and slurry management is associated with several environmental im- pacts. The principal impact categories affected by animal slurry management are; (a) climate change potential, (b) acidification potential, mainly induced by ammonia (NH3) emissions, (c) eutro- phication potential, and (d) the use of fossil resources (De Vries et al., 2012; Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009). Potential impacts of live- stock production on the surrounding environment that are

generally not reported in life cycle studies relate to emissions of odorous compounds, microbes, antibiotics, hormones and heavy metals such as copper and zinc (Berenguer et al., 2008; Blanes- Vidal et al., 2009; Hansen et al., 2012; Igel-Egalon et al., 2012; Klein et al., 2010; Mantovi et al., 2003). The environmental impacts are greater in areas with high live- stock densities, where field nutrient application of slurry may approach or exceed the capacity of the crops. In the EU15, such areas can be found e.g. in The Netherlands, Belgium, western France, the UK, Ireland, northern Italy, north-west Germany and Denmark (Grizzetti et al., 2007). Concern about the consequences of nitrate (NO) leaching in such areas has led national and EU authorities to limit the application rate of slurry N. Phosphorus surpluses from excess slurry application also create a risk of P losses (MacDonald et al., 2011) and are a concern in relation to
implementation of the EU Water Framework Directive (Council Directive 2000/60/EC of 23 October 23 2000).

In contrast, in areas with low livestock densities, agricultural fields receive insufficient amounts of nutrients from slurry to maintain good crop yields and farmers need to apply supplemen- tary mineral fertilisers. Non-renewable natural resources, such as phosphate rock, oil and natural gas are used for the production of N and P mineral fertiliser and considerable impacts are related to the extraction, manufacture and use of these fertilisers. A geographical redistribution of animal slurry nutrients would enable a reduction in the consumption of mineral fertiliser in areas with low livestock densities and would lower environmental impacts in areas with high livestock densities. Increasing oil prices will increase the price of N mineral fertiliser, so there will probably be an economic incentive to utilise slurry N more effectively in the future. In addition, in areas where current or future EU or national environ- mental legislation imposes restrictions on the application of slurry to land, farmers face limitations on their current or future livestock production and there may be economic benefits to be gained from a geographical redistribution of slurry nutrients.

To facilitate nutrient redistribution, a range of different technolo- gies have been or are currently being developed. The technology most widely applied at present is slurry separation, where the slurry is separated into a solid and a liquid fraction (Hjorth et al., 2010; Moller et al., 2007). Concentrating a proportion of the nutrients into a less bulky solid fraction reduces the cost of transporting these nutrients to areas where they can be utilised more effectively. Several separation methods are available. Screw press separation is a relatively cheap method that produces a liquid fraction containing most of the water and easily available N and potassium (K), but less than half of the P (Hjorth et al., 2010). Centrifuge separation is more expensive but partitions most P to the solid fraction (Hjorth et al., 2010). The solid fraction contains a high concentration of organic N and organic and mineral P. Agricultural land near livestock production units has often received animal slurry for many years, which means that P concen- trations in the soil are already sufficiently high to satisfy crop demand (Grizzetti et al., 2007). Other technologies that have been developed to facilitate the redistribution of nutrients include NH3 stripping from the liquid fraction (Alitalo 2012), which allows a redistribution of inorganic N and at the same time reduces NH3 emissions. Finally, composting of the solid fraction (Park et al., 2011; Bustamante et al., 2012), stabilises this fraction and reduces its volume, making it easier and less odorous to store and transport.

The advantages of redistributing nutrients have to be weighed against the environmental impacts associated with the technology. In this study, a life cycle assessment (LCA) approach was used to determine the environmental impact potential of different slurry treatment technologies and compare them with conventional slurry management.

8: Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a methodology which can be used to compare resource consumption and environmental impacts of products or services providing the same function (JRC-IEA, 2010). The LCA framework and methodology used here comply with the ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 standards (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006). A consequential approach was used, i.e. with substitution of by-products, use of marginal data for electricity and mineral fertil- iser, and examination of life cycle processes that were expected to be different for the systems studied. The LCA was based on information from the literature and a systems analysis of the treatment tech- nologies (Hutchings et al., 2013), including simulations with the

Farm-N model (Hutchings, 2010). The LCA itself was conducted us- ing GaBi software (www.pe-international.com). The functional unit that formed the basis for assessment was management of 1000 kg of slurry excreted by pigs.
9: The geographical scope for the assessment was Denmark. This means that technologies most often used in Denmark were modelled. The technologies for slurry treatment analysed are currently used in Denmark or have high potential applicability. Emissions from slurry, liquid and solid fractions were analysed from the moment the slurry was excreted by pigs until 20 years after field application, as modelled by the Farm-N model (Hutchings, 2010). The effects of all emissions were considered for a period of 100 years from the moment they reached water bodies or the atmosphere. There are differing views in the literature on whether and how to include biogenic CO2 emissions in LCA. In most studies on slurry treatment, biogenic CO2 is excluded (e.g. De Vries et al., 2012; Lopez- Ridaura et al., 2009). The positions taken by the relevant standards are not entirely consistent. The ISO 14044 standard does not provide guidance on the issue of biogenic emissions (ISO 14044, 2006). US EPA (2011) states that there should not be categorical rules about inclusion or exclusion of biogenic carbon (C). PAS2050 suggests that biogenic CO2 emissions should be inventoried, but that characteri- sation is optional. ILCD and the draft PEF (JRC-IEA, 2010, 2012) state that both biogenic emissions and biogenic uptake should be re- ported separately from non-biogenic flows and that characterisa- tion factors should be applied. In order to assess the impact of inclusion of biogenic CO2 on results and to analyse soil C changes, biogenic CO2 was included in the inventory analysis in the present study. Furthermore, the life cycle impact assessment was performed twice; including and excluding biogenic CO2.
10: This study considered four technologies for redistributing slurry nutrients from a pig-producing ('donor') farm to fields on a 'recipient' farm and compared them with a reference scenario:

<list>Reference scenario: Conventional slurry management, including in-house storage, external storage and application on the donor farm. 
Screw press scenario: Slurry separation with a mechanical screw press, with donor farm application of the liquid fraction, and transportation and application on a recipient farm of the solid fraction. 
Screw press with composting scenario: As for the screw press scenario, but with composting of the solid fraction (with the addition of a bulking agent), then transportation to a recipient farm. 
Decanter centrifuge scenario: Slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge, with donor farm application of the liquid fraction, and transportation and application on a recipient farm of the solid fraction. 
Decanter centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario: As for the decanter centrifuge scenario, but with NH3 stripping of the liquid fraction resulting in a compact N fertiliser (ammonium sulphate), and a rejectate with very low N content.</list>
11: The reference scenario was based on conventional slurry man- agement in Denmark, with in-house storage for 6 weeks, covered outdoor storage for 6 months and field application with a slurry tanker. It was assumed that the pigs consumed an average Danish

feed diet and that the pig houses had partially slatted flooring. All liquid slurry (untreated slurry and liquid fraction) was assumed to be stored in covered tanks. The solid fraction was assumed to be covered with an air-tight lining during storage to reduce aerobic self-heating, but compost was assumed to be well aerated in long rows (windrows) turned every second week.

Liquid slurry was assumed to be applied by a trailing hose system to fields on the donor farm, with an average road distance of 8 km from storage. It was assumed that liquid manure replaced the mineral N fertiliser ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3). Solid fractions were assumed to be transported to areas where P is needed on a recipient farm, over an average distance of 100 km. As farmers on these fields with low P status would normally apply mineral P fertiliser, it was assumed that the solid fraction replaced both the mineral P fertiliser single superphosphate and the mineral N fertiliser ammonium ni- trate. Solid fractions were assumed to be applied by broadcast spreading, followed by rapid incorporation. The ammonium sulphate produced during NH3 stripping was assumed to be transported over a distance of 100 km and applied by injector. Phosphorus losses from the soil were assumed to be 5% of the difference between the Papplied as fertiliser and the average amount of P harvested in crops (assumed to be 21.5 kg P/ha and year), also called P surplus. When less P was applied than removed with harvested crops, this was modelled as a negative emission of P, as part of the soil P reserves can be considered to be used by plants. This was favourable on the donor farm, as these fields were assumed to be oversupplied with P in the past.
12: The reference scenario was based on conventional slurry man- agement in Denmark, with in-house storage for 6 weeks, covered outdoor storage for 6 months and field application with a slurry tanker. It was assumed that the pigs consumed an average Danish

feed diet and that the pig houses had partially slatted flooring. All liquid slurry (untreated slurry and liquid fraction) was assumed to be stored in covered tanks. The solid fraction was assumed to be covered with an air-tight lining during storage to reduce aerobic self-heating, but compost was assumed to be well aerated in long rows (windrows) turned every second week.

Liquid slurry was assumed to be applied by a trailing hose system to fields on the donor farm, with an average road distance of 8 km from storage. It was assumed that liquid manure replaced the mineral N fertiliser ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3). Solid fractions were assumed to be transported to areas where P is needed on a recipient farm, over an average distance of 100 km. As farmers on these fields with low P status would normally apply mineral P fertiliser, it was assumed that the solid fraction replaced both the mineral P fertiliser single superphosphate and the mineral N fertiliser ammonium ni- trate. Solid fractions were assumed to be applied by broadcast spreading, followed by rapid incorporation. The ammonium sulphate produced during NH3 stripping was assumed to be transported over a distance of 100 km and applied by injector. Phosphorus losses from the soil were assumed to be 5% of the difference between the Papplied as fertiliser and the average amount of P harvested in crops (assumed to be 21.5 kg P/ha and year), also called P surplus. When less P was applied than removed with harvested crops, this was modelled as a negative emission of P, as part of the soil P reserves can be considered to be used by plants. This was favourable on the donor farm, as these fields were assumed to be oversupplied with P in the past.
13: Fig. 1 provides an overview of the system boundaries used in the study. Processes not included were: pig production and its related impacts; potassium, sulphur and micronutrient fertiliser replace- ment; and the physical buildings and material used for storage and application of slurry fractions.

21: Windrow composting was assumed to be used in the screw press with composting scenario, with rape straw used as a bulking agent. It was assumed that if this rape straw had not been used for composting, it would have been left on the field after rapeseed harvesting, but that its N content would not replace mineral fer- tiliser. Rape straw is not included in the fertiliser accounts that Danish farmers have to complete annually to fulfil the re- quirements of regulations enacted to enforce the EU Nitrate Directive (Council Directive 91/676/EEC of 12 December 1991). The compost was assumed to be turned every second week for a period of 6 months (Amlinger et al., 2008), with an assumed energy de- mand of 0.27 L diesel per tonne of compost.
22: The dry matter content in the liquid fraction after centrifuge separation is sufficiently low for NH3 stripping to be applicable. The pH of the liquid fraction is first increased from around 7 to 11 by adding lime (calcium oxide). The liquid fraction is then heated to 70 °C by a natural gas boiler, using 61 kWh per 1000 kg liquid fraction, before it enters the stripper. In the stripper, the heated and pH-adjusted liquid fraction is passed over a structured packing material, creating a thin film with a considerable surface area, and brought into contact with an opposing flow of air, whereby NH3

volatilises and is transported away by the air flow. Ammonia released from the stripping column is collected using wet scrub- bing in an absorber, where the gas reacts with sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and an ammonium sulphate ((NH4)2SO4) solution is formed (Christensen et al., 2013). The electricity demand for NH3 stripping was assumed to be 12 kWh per 1000 kg of liquid fraction (Hutchings et al., 2013).

Marginal data were used for mineral fertiliser (ammonium ni- trate and single superphosphate) and electricity due to the conse- quential approach employed in the study. The mineral fertiliser equivalencies (MFE) of slurry products were based on the values prescribed by the Danish slurry and fertiliser standards (The Danish Plant Directorate, 2009). For slurry, MFE is 75%, implying that 1 kg of N in slurry ex-storage replaces 0.75 kg N in NH4NO3; for the other fractions, see Table 2. There are currently no regulations on P fer- tiliser in Denmark. The amount of mineral P fertiliser replaced by the solid slurry fractions was based on annual crop demand of 21.5 kg/ha. It was assumed that the P in slurry is as effective as the P in single superphosphate, resulting in one-to-one replacement, i.e. MFE for P of 100%. This does not correspond with reality in the short-term, but is probably likely to be a good estimate on longer time scales (Huang et al., 2011). Marginal electricity in Denmark was assumed to be produced by combustion of hard coal.

29: Assessment of the climate change potential included CO2 (either non-biogenic only or both non-biogenic and biogenic), CH4 and N20 (both direct and indirect) emissions. For the freshwater eutrophication potential it included phosphate emissions, for the marine eutrophication potential it included NO3 and NH3 emis- sions and for the terrestrial acidification potential it included NH3 emissions. The fossil resource use included two types of fossil re- sources: phosphate rock and fossil energy resources (crude oil, hard coal, lignite and natural gas). The life cycle impact assessment method used for analysis of environmental impact potentials was ReCiPe2008 (Goedkoop et al., 2009). Finally, an assessment of soil C, N and P storage was conducted. Carbon sequestration was calcu- lated using the simple, dynamic C-Tool model (Hutchings, 2010). Nitrogen sequestration was calculated using an assumed C:N ratio of 10 for humus organic matter.
30: A sensitivity analysis was carried out to examine the influence of changes in the selected parameters. A total of 11 parameters were varied (Table 3). In addition soil type was changed to a coarse loamy sand soil, coarse sandy soil and fine sandy clay loam soil. The lowest and highest reasonable values from the literature were used for the sensitivity analysis. Only parameters that had the largest influence on the results are described in the results section.
31: The climate change potential of the scenarios is shown in Fig. 2, where negative values represent potential savings from substituted mineral fertiliser and from sequestration of C in soil. The climate change potential showed small variations between the reference and treatment scenarios. Net climate change potential was higher in all scenarios when biogenic C was included than when it was excluded. The difference was mainly due to field emissions of CO2 and C sequestration in soil. However, the scenarios adopted the same relative order irrespective of inclusion of biogenic C, and therefore this proved not to be decisive.

All treatment scenarios, except centrifuge with NH3 stripping, showed a smaller difference in climate change potential than the reference scenario when biogenic C was included. The greenhouse gas emissions were higher during NH3 stripping, but these were partly compensated for by an overall higher mineral fertiliser replacement efficiency for the centrifuge with NH3 stripping sce- nario. Greenhouse gas emissions during composting contributed to the high climate change potential of the screw press with com- posting scenario.

35: Large differences were observed for freshwater eutrophication potential (Fig. 3). The reference scenario had the highest net impact potential, while that of the screw press and screw press with composting scenarios was 24% lower than in the reference. This was due to the assumed replacement of mineral P fertiliser from the solid fraction after separation (shown as a negative impact poten- tial in Fig. 3), and thus a positive effect of nutrient redistribution. The centrifuge and centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenarios gave an even lower net freshwater eutrophication potential. A large share of P was partitioned to the solid fraction after centrifuge separation, resulting in a large mineral P fertiliser replacement level on the recipient farm and thus again a positive effect of nutrient redistri- bution. However, the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario had a higher impact potential than the centrifuge scenario without NH3 stripping. This was caused by the difference between the liquid fraction and the rejectate. 3: The general trend for marine eutrophication potential was for the treatment scenarios to show a net impact potential 30-40% lower than in the reference scenario (Fig. 4). Field emissions generated the main positive contribution in all scenarios, but a large share of this was offset by avoided field emissions from substituted use of mineral fertilisers. When only the field emissions of slurry fractions were considered, the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario had the highest impact potential. However, the net impact potential of this scenario was the lowest of all slurry treatments, because it also had the highest negative impact po- tential, as the largest proportion of mineral fertiliser was replaced.
4: The housing stage is the main contributor to the terrestrial acidification potential (Fig. 5). However, the impact from this stage was identical for all scenarios, as the different treatment technol- logies are employed after slurry has been pumped out of the housing system. Negative values in the figure represent potential savings from substituted mineral fertiliser. When the terrestrial acidification potential from the housing stage was excluded from the analysis, the impact potential from the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario was 44% lower than in the reference scenario. All other treatment scenarios were similar to the reference scenario.
Terrestrial eutrophication showed the same pattern as terres- trial acidification. Emissions of NH3 were the main contributor to both terrestrial acidification and terrestrial eutrophication.

3.6. Soil carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus storage
All other things being equal, C sequestration in the soil depends on the amount of C added. Since the excretion of C from the pigs was the same for all scenarios, any differences between the
scenarios in C sequestration were due to differences in the amounts of C lost during slurry management.

The total area fertilised on the donor and recipient farms as a result of the functional unit of 1000 kg slurry was approximately equal in the reference and treatment scenarios. It ranged from 321 m² for the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario to 330 m² for the screw press with composting scenario (Table 4). The soil C, N and P storage values given in Table 4 represent changes in storage over 20 years as a consequence of a single application of slurry fractions instead of mineral fertilisers. Soil C and N storage mainly occurred on fields where solid fractions were applied, but these areas were small compared with the areas where liquid fractions were applied. The weighted average of C and N storage, based on area, was similar in most treatment scenarios and the reference scenario. Only the centrifuge scenario with NH3 stripping had lower soil C storage, due to the ammonium sulphate decreasing the soil C pool.

For net soil P storage, only slurry in the reference and liquid fractions in the treatment scenarios had impacts. This was due to the assumption that P in the solid fraction replaced mineral P fer- tiliser with a replacement efficiency of 100% (i.e. the net impact was 0). The screw press scenarios showed similar P storage to the reference scenario. The centrifuge scenario showed negative P storage because plant P uptake was higher than P application. The centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario showed high P storage due to application of rejectate in accordance with N restrictions. The rejectate had a low N content, as most mineral N ended up in the ammonium sulphate produced during stripping. However, the P content in the rejectate was high, resulting in over-fertilisation with P.

3.6. Soil carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus storage
All other things being equal, C sequestration in the soil depends on the amount of C added. Since the excretion of C from the pigs was the same for all scenarios, any differences between the
scenarios in C sequestration were due to differences in the amounts of C lost during slurry management.

The total area fertilised on the donor and recipient farms as a result of the functional unit of 1000 kg slurry was approximately equal in the reference and treatment scenarios. The soil C, N and P storage values given in Table 4 represent changes in storage over 20 years as a consequence of a single application of slurry fractions instead of mineral fertilisers. Soil C and N storage mainly occurred on fields where solid fractions were applied, but these areas were small compared with the areas where liquid fractions were applied. The weighted average of C and N storage, based on area, was similar in most treatment scenarios and the reference scenario. Only the centrifuge scenario with NH3 stripping had lower soil C storage, due to the ammonium sulphate decreasing the soil C pool.

For net soil P storage, only slurry in the reference and liquid fractions in the treatment scenarios had impacts. This was due to the assumption that P in the solid fraction replaced mineral P fer- tiliser with a replacement efficiency of 100% (i.e. the net impact was 0). The screw press scenarios showed similar P storage to the reference scenario. The centrifuge scenario showed negative P storage because plant P uptake was higher than P application. The centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario showed high P storage due to application of rejectate in accordance with N restrictions. The rejectate had a low N content, as most mineral N ended up in the ammonium sulphate produced during stripping. However, the P content in the rejectate was high, resulting in over-fertilisation with P.

15: <header>3.7.1. Emissions coefficients for ammonia from the field </header>

For emissions of NH3 from the field, a set of low and high emissions coefficients were analysed in addition to the standard coefficients (Table 3). Adjusting the emissions coefficients for NH3 from the field influenced both marine eutrophication and terres- trial acidification potential. When NH3 emissions coefficients were low, less mineral N was emitted as NH3 but more mineral N remained in the soil, where it is prone to leaching after conversion to NO. The change in marine eutrophication potential for low NH3 emissions coefficients compared with the standard values ranged from 14% for the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario to 50% for the screw press with composting scenario and the centrifuge sce- nario (error bars in Fig. 7). The corresponding change in terrestrial acidification potential ranged from -10% for the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario to 29% for the screw press with composting scenario (error bars in Fig. 7). When NH3 emissions coefficients were high, more mineral N was emitted as NH3 but less mineral N was lost by leaching. Thus the change in marine eutrophication potential for high NH3 emissions coefficients compared with the standard values ranged from -1.6% for the screw press scenario to 14% for the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario. The cor- responding changes in terrestrial acidification potential ranged from 2.5% for the screw press scenario to 10% for the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario. The relative ranking of scenarios did not change for marine eutrophication, but did change for terrestrial acidification, with the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario having the lowest impact potential with standard values, but the highest impact potential with low emissions coefficients for NH3.
16: <header>3.7.2. Emissions coefficient for nitrous oxide from the field </header>

The standard coefficient for nitrous oxide emissions from the field is 2% of total N applied, with a range of 0.7-6% (IPCC, 2006b). In the sensitivity analysis, the emissions coefficients were varied within this range (error bars in Fig. 8). However, only climate change potential was affected and hence only these results are shown. Decreasing the emissions coefficient from 2% to 0.7% resulted in a decrease in climate change potential of 31% for screw press with composting and 48% for the centrifuge scenario. Increasing the emissions coefficient from 2% to 6% resulted in an increase in climate change potential of 94% for the screw press with composting scenario and 148% for the centrifuge scenario. How- ever, the relative order of the scenarios did not change.
17: <header>3.7.3. Emissions coefficient for phosphorus from the field </header>

The standard emissions coefficient of P from the field used here was 5% of the P surplus, but there is great uncertainty about this (Nielsen and Wenzel, 2007; Peters et al., 2011; Sharpley and Moyer, 2000). The approach of Nielsen and Wenzel (2007), in which the emissions coefficient ranges from 0% to 100% of the P surplus, was chosen for the sensitivity analysis. Only freshwater eutrophication potential was affected and therefore only these results are shown in Fig. 9. Increasing the emissions coefficient from 5% to 100% of P surplus increased the savings in freshwater eutrophication poten- tial for the centrifuge scenario by a factor of 15 (error bars in Fig. 9). The centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario showed the highest increase, by a factor of 25. The impact potential of the other sce- narios increased by a factor of approximately 20. The relative order of the scenarios did not change.

Vries et al., 2012; Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009), unless bio-energy is produced (Hamelin et al., 2011; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010a). As regards fossil resource depletion, the reference scenario had the lowest fossil resource use of all scenarios, since the higher energy demand for the various treatments could not be compensated for by substituted production of mineral fertilisers. This confirmed findings by De Vries et al. (2012), Peters and Rowley (2009) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009). As regards terrestrial acidification po- tential, the treatment scenarios were similar to the reference sce- nario, whereas the screw press with composting scenario and the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario gave a higher climate change potential than the reference. A higher climate change po- tential for composting was also observed by Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) and for NH3 stripping by De Vries et al. (2012). The differ- ence between the reference and treatment scenarios was largest for

freshwater and marine eutrophication potential, for which all treatment scenarios gave a lower value. In contrast, Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) found a higher eutrophication potential in the treat- ment scenario. Two main differences between our study and that of Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) are the inclusion of NO3 emissions in our study and the assumption that P in the solid fraction replaced mineral P fertiliser on the recipient farm but not P in slurry, liquid and rejectate, (due to the assumed high soil P status on the donor farm).

There were no consistent trends in emissions during storage and after field application of the different fractions. The large variability in literature data is due to influences of specific circumstances, e.g. storage type, storage time, field application mode, soil type and climatological conditions. As shown by the sensitivity analysis, the parameters with the largest relative influence on the results when

varied within a reasonable range were the coefficients for NH3, N2O and P emissions from the field. IPCC (2006b) stated a factor of almost 9 for the uncertainty in the coefficient for field emissions of N20 from animal manure or urine in the Tier 1 method applied in our study. As our results proved sensitive to the value of this emissions coefficient, it would be an advantage to use a higher Tier method. N application rate, fertiliser type, crop type, soil C content, soil texture, drainage and soil pH can all influence N20 emissions (Bouwman et al., 2002). Better and more specific data on N20 emissions in Denmark could minimise the uncertainty and result in more accurate model outputs. Emissions coefficients for P from the field are difficult to quantify, as there are many pathways by which P can be lost and many factors that can influence P mobilisation in soil, e.g. soil moisture, temperature, surface physico-chemical properties, and soil pH and redox potential (Shen et al., 2011). Soil P concentrations have an influence on potential P losses, with high P levels bringing a higher risk of losses (McDowell et al., 2001). Therefore, soil P levels might be a better proxy for freshwater eutrophication than proportion of surplus application of slurry, as used in this study. Phosphorus emissions are often not explicitly mentioned in LCA studies on treatment of pig slurry, due to a lack of data (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009) or due to the assumption that P in slurry fractions replaces mineral P fertiliser with an efficiency of 100% (De Vries et al., 2012; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010b). Hamelin et al. (2011) assumed that the solid fraction after treatment was applied to a P-deficient soil, which resulted in mineral P fertiliser replacement. A similar assumption was made in this study for the recipient farm and this yielded the same response in freshwater eutrophication potential.

The ranking of the scenarios did not change in the sensitivity analysis with changes in coefficients, except for terrestrial acidi- fication with low NH3 emissions coefficients from the field. When slurry was assumed to be applied in accordance with N regula- tions, the amount of P exceeded crop uptake in some scenarios, especially the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario. The rejec- tate was applied on a small area, owing to its low N content, but its relatively high P content then resulted in over-fertilisation of P on fields with high existing P levels. The assumption of rejectate application corresponding to maximum N regulations might not represent realistic farm practice in Denmark. Rejectate application rate in accordance with P uptake (21.5 kg P/ha) would increase the area required for application from 36 m² (Table 4) to 155 but P losses from this fraction would decrease to 0. Nitrogen application would be 39 kg/ha, but as the total amount of N in the applied rejectate is the same regardless of application limit, the total amount of mineral N fertiliser replaced would also remain the same, giving no effect on other impact categories. This means that at more realistic rejectate application rates, the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario would have a lower net impact. The solid fraction also had a relatively high P content compared with the N content, resulting in a P application around 10 times higher than crop uptake. Assuming that this is a one-time application, it could be seen as a P boost for soils on the recipient farm (assumed to have low P status) and would result in high residual P availability in subsequent years. When using slurry treatment technologies, it is thus important to take P limits and P levels in soil into consid- eration. A net under-application of P occurred in the centrifuge scenario when the liquid fraction was applied on the donor farm, but this did not lead to immediate P deficiency, as the P level in these fields was assumed to be high and able to sustain crop P demand for several years. In this scenario, valuable P could be utilised on a field with P deficiency, instead of accumulating more P in already saturated soils. The value of P application is thus highly dependent on the P status of soil, as successfully modelled in the current LCA.

We did not include potassium (K) in the study. However, in all the scenarios considered, most K would have been retained on the donor farm and would have led to over-fertilisation with this element. Copper and zinc are also excluded. These elements are added to pig feed in order to stimulate growth, with most being excreted. Copper and zinc are conserved in the slurry manage- ment scenarios considered here, so are applied to the soil in the slurry products. The status of the soil to which the slurry is applied is therefore important. Copper and zinc are micronutrients that plants need for growth (White and Zasoski, 1999), so application to fields where they are deficient could stimulate plant growth. However, these elements are toxic at higher concentrations and accumulation in the soil as a result of repeated applications of slurry or slurry fractions could lead to problems with food and feed contamination (Berenguer et al., 2008; Mantovi et al., 2003). Exporting copper and zinc from farms where the soil already has high levels could therefore be highly advantageous. The bioavailability of trace elements is a function of factors including pH (Chirenje et al., 2002), redox conditions (Peters et al., 1997), clay minerals (Li, 2006) and organic matter (Acosta et al., 2011). The separation efficiencies for these elements are quite high (Popovic et al., 2012), so the potential of slurry separation to reduce copper and zinc loadings on pig farms merits further investigation. Finally, odorous emissions are very important in connection with slurry management technologies. However, these emissions are excluded from the current study because detection methods have not been developed. Appropriate methods are ur- gently needed.

freshwater and marine eutrophication potential, for which all treatment scenarios gave a lower value. In contrast, Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) found a higher eutrophication potential in the treat- ment scenario. Two main differences between our study and that of Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) are the inclusion of NO3 emissions in our study and the assumption that P in the solid fraction replaced mineral P fertiliser on the recipient farm but not P in slurry, liquid and rejectate, (due to the assumed high soil P status on the donor farm).

There were no consistent trends in emissions during storage and after field application of the different fractions. The large variability in literature data is due to influences of specific circumstances, e.g. storage type, storage time, field application mode, soil type and climatological conditions. As shown by the sensitivity analysis, the parameters with the largest relative influence on the results when

varied within a reasonable range were the coefficients for NH3, N2O and P emissions from the field. IPCC (2006b) stated a factor of almost 9 for the uncertainty in the coefficient for field emissions of N20 from animal manure or urine in the Tier 1 method applied in our study. As our results proved sensitive to the value of this emissions coefficient, it would be an advantage to use a higher Tier method. N application rate, fertiliser type, crop type, soil C content, soil texture, drainage and soil pH can all influence N20 emissions (Bouwman et al., 2002). Better and more specific data on N20 emissions in Denmark could minimise the uncertainty and result in more accurate model outputs. Emissions coefficients for P from the field are difficult to quantify, as there are many pathways by which P can be lost and many factors that can influence P mobilisation in soil, e.g. soil moisture, temperature, surface physico-chemical properties, and soil pH and redox potential (Shen et al., 2011). Soil P concentrations have an influence on potential P losses, with high P levels bringing a higher risk of losses (McDowell et al., 2001). Therefore, soil P levels might be a better proxy for freshwater eutrophication than proportion of surplus application of slurry, as used in this study. Phosphorus emissions are often not explicitly mentioned in LCA studies on treatment of pig slurry, due to a lack of data (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009) or due to the assumption that P in slurry fractions replaces mineral P fertiliser with an efficiency of 100% (De Vries et al., 2012; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010b). Hamelin et al. (2011) assumed that the solid fraction after treatment was applied to a P-deficient soil, which resulted in mineral P fertiliser replacement. A similar assumption was made in this study for the recipient farm and this yielded the same response in freshwater eutrophication potential.

The ranking of the scenarios did not change in the sensitivity analysis with changes in coefficients, except for terrestrial acidi- fication with low NH3 emissions coefficients from the field. When slurry was assumed to be applied in accordance with N regula- tions, the amount of P exceeded crop uptake in some scenarios, especially the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario. The rejec- tate was applied on a small area, owing to its low N content, but its relatively high P content then resulted in over-fertilisation of P on fields with high existing P levels. The assumption of rejectate application corresponding to maximum N regulations might not represent realistic farm practice in Denmark. Rejectate application rate in accordance with P uptake (21.5 kg P/ha) would increase the area required for application from 36 m² (Table 4) to 155 but P losses from this fraction would decrease to 0. Nitrogen application would be 39 kg/ha, but as the total amount of N in the applied rejectate is the same regardless of application limit, the total amount of mineral N fertiliser replaced would also remain the same, giving no effect on other impact categories. This means that at more realistic rejectate application rates, the centrifuge with NH3 stripping scenario would have a lower net impact. The solid fraction also had a relatively high P content compared with the N content, resulting in a P application around 10 times higher than crop uptake. Assuming that this is a one-time application, it could be seen as a P boost for soils on the recipient farm (assumed to have low P status) and would result in high residual P availability in subsequent years. When using slurry treatment technologies, it is thus important to take P limits and P levels in soil into consid- eration. A net under-application of P occurred in the centrifuge scenario when the liquid fraction was applied on the donor farm, but this did not lead to immediate P deficiency, as the P level in these fields was assumed to be high and able to sustain crop P demand for several years. In this scenario, valuable P could be utilised on a field with P deficiency, instead of accumulating more P in already saturated soils. The value of P application is thus highly dependent on the P status of soil, as successfully modelled in the current LCA.

We did not include potassium (K) in the study. However, in all the scenarios considered, most K would have been retained on the donor farm and would have led to over-fertilisation with this element. Copper and zinc are also excluded. These elements are added to pig feed in order to stimulate growth, with most being excreted. Copper and zinc are conserved in the slurry manage- ment scenarios considered here, so are applied to the soil in the slurry products. The status of the soil to which the slurry is applied is therefore important. Copper and zinc are micronutrients that plants need for growth (White and Zasoski, 1999), so application to fields where they are deficient could stimulate plant growth. However, these elements are toxic at higher concentrations and accumulation in the soil as a result of repeated applications of slurry or slurry fractions could lead to problems with food and feed contamination (Berenguer et al., 2008; Mantovi et al., 2003). Exporting copper and zinc from farms where the soil already has high levels could therefore be highly advantageous. The bioavailability of trace elements is a function of factors including pH (Chirenje et al., 2002), redox conditions (Peters et al., 1997), clay minerals (Li, 2006) and organic matter (Acosta et al., 2011). The separation efficiencies for these elements are quite high (Popovic et al., 2012), so the potential of slurry separation to reduce copper and zinc loadings on pig farms merits further investigation. Finally, odorous emissions are very important in connection with slurry management technologies. However, these emissions are excluded from the current study because detection methods have not been developed. Appropriate methods are ur- gently needed.

25: In general, the environmental impact potentials of the slurry treatment technologies analysed here were equal to or lower than those of the reference. Treatment by centrifuge separation had the lowest environmental impact potential. As regards climate change, freshwater eutrophication and fossil resource use, centrifuge separation alone was better than centrifuge with NH3 stripping, while the latter was better in terms of terrestrial acid- ification and marine eutrophication. Composting either slightly increased or decreased environmental impacts, depending on the impact category considered. Key parameters with the largest in- fluence on the results were the coefficients used for NH3, N20 and P emissions from the field. The values reported in the literature vary widely, largely reflecting the fact that they depend strongly on the specific conditions under which slurry is stored and applied. More accurate results could be achieved with inclusion of more specific assumptions concerning the conditions under which slurry is applied and with more complex modelling of their effect on emissions. However, this would reduce the general applicability of the LCA results. Although centrifuge separation tended to have the lowest impact potential, the advantages were relatively small. We there- fore concluded that no single treatment option for geographically redistributing slurry nutrients is clearly superior with respect to potential environmental impacts. Thus, the choice of appropriate technology depends on other considerations, such as local policy or regulatory priorities regarding environmental impacts, cost, odour or practicality.
